description: Index of Socrates' role and speaking presence across all texts in the corpus. Texts are categorized by the degree of Socrates' participation.

primary_speaker.description: Socrates is the main speaker or narrator, dominating the conversation

texts.notes: Defense speech. Socrates speaks almost continuously, with brief cross-examination of Meletus.
Dialogue with Crito. Includes the extended prosopopoeia of the Laws of Athens.
Socrates leads discussion on death and immortality. Phaedo narrates the frame.
Socrates questions Euthyphro about the nature of piety.
Socrates leads. Includes the slave boy geometry demonstration.
Socrates and Protagoras share roughly equal time. Socrates narrates the frame.
Socrates engages three interlocutors in sequence: Gorgias, Polus, Callicles.
Socrates narrates the entire dialogue and leads the conversation across all 10 books.
Socrates delivers one of seven speeches on love, plus reports Diotima's teaching. Apollodorus narrates.
Socrates delivers two speeches on love and leads the discussion of rhetoric.
Socrates leads the inquiry into knowledge, playing intellectual midwife.
Socrates leads the discussion on pleasure vs. knowledge.
Socrates delivers extensive etymological analyses.
Socrates questions Ion about the nature of rhapsodic art.
Socrates questions Hippias about beauty, also role-playing a 'tough questioner'.
Socrates argues the paradox that the voluntary wrongdoer is better.
Socrates leads inquiry into courage with Laches and Nicias.
Socrates examines Charmides and Critias on temperance.
Socrates discusses friendship with young boys at a palaestra.
Socrates narrates but the sophist brothers take significant speaking time.
Socrates delivers an extended funeral oration.
Socrates questions young Alcibiades about self-knowledge and political ambition.
Collection of Socratic conversations. Socrates is the central speaker throughout.
Socrates' words at and around the trial, as reported by Xenophon.
Socrates is a prominent participant but shares the conversation with many guests.
Socrates discusses household management, then reports Ischomachus's teachings.

present_but_not_leading.description: Socrates is present in the dialogue but is not the primary speaker

texts.notes: Socrates speaks in the opening exchange, then the Eleatic Stranger leads the conversation. Socrates is present but silent.
Socrates speaks very briefly. The Eleatic Stranger leads the discussion with Young Socrates (a different character).
Socrates speaks at the opening, summarizing the previous day's discussion. Timaeus then delivers the cosmological monologue.
Socrates is present but barely speaks. Critias narrates the Atlantis story.
Young Socrates speaks significantly in Part I (discussing Forms with Parmenides). In Part II, Parmenides leads an extended dialectical exercise.
Clitophon does most of the speaking, criticizing Socrates. Socrates barely responds.
Socrates is a significant character but the play centers on Strepsiades. This is a satirical portrayal — Aristophanes' Socrates is a composite of sophists and natural philosophers.
